{"text":"Shinde, solar vr evda focus nai karayla pahije...solar PVs banvayla je material lagta te swatah carbide/nitride and sulphide intensive ahe...plus tyachi life 20-25 years max..so parat 20 years nantr PV change kara...yes it is any day better than coal and thermal but lifecycle carbon footprint still exists, so not completely sustainable...apart from that, a lot of land /surface area is required for these projects...india madhe adhich jamini che vande, tyat navin jamin anaychi kuthun? Plus indian climate is not optimal for solar PV operation, optimal temperature is between 18-24°C where PVs operate at highest efficiency and have longer lifecycles, indian summers where temperatures reach 40-50°C are not ideal for PV operation, after certain years of operations O&M costs would increase exponentially since circuits would melt, short circuits would increase etc, other option is CSV for India where we can actually take advantage of the heat...but that would create heat island effect...we need to rethink our solar strategy"}

{"text":"Don't want to be a pessimist, but Solar panel (pv) manufacturing is an energy intensive task plus it takes a huge toll on the environment...plus, the Sun belt is not that suitable for solar pvs since the temperature range is on the higher side and pvs operate better under cooler climatic conditions (between 15-25°C for most efficient generation around 18-20%)... Concentrated Solar Power (CSP) is probably a suitable option since it is based on heat and we can use this tech for maximum solar power in the Sun belt, but again it would be costly and we would be exposing the environment to lot of heavy metals...India for now should invest in material sciences to find a suitable material that can give us solar energy at the highest efficiency, and can be produced locally with minimum environmental impact...pervoskite is one possible option to give efficient generation at high temperatures but again it has expensive heavy metals...but yes, this discussion is needed in India and Indian Colleges should ask their students to invest time in research rather than just obtain a degree"}

{"text":"In India's latest national budget, motorcycles with engines over 350 CC capacity would fall under higher tax rate, so the domestic indian market is going to take some time to adjust to this. Not that there is any sudden reduction in demand for 350+ capacity bikes in India, but companies are going to hold their horses back a little when it comes to launching new platforms using their domestic brand names (at least until the spending capacity of Indian consumers normalizes again), rather they would rely on foreign brand partners or acquisitions to launch newer platforms with higher capacities with joint production and/or sale in India and for Indian export markets like Africa and South America. Since this market is still small compared to India's domestic market, i would not expect a a 600cc or 750cc mid bikes coming below the price of R130,000 at the moment, because economies of scale would not justify it. However, in the next 3-5 years, when Indian spending capacity crosses a certain threshold (say $3500 per capita gdp), you would see a whole new variety in mid size bike segments, maybe at an even lower price point."}

{"text":"This video is made with an inherent bias. India doesn't need to show off economic growth with skyscrapers. 2-5 FSI for cities is more than enough. India too has ancient cities with its own natural aesthetics hidden under all the dust of construction, illegal billboards, wiring etc. We just need to reorganize our cities in a better way.
